name: Edgar Sampson
description: American jazz composer, arranger, saxophonist, and violinist (1907–1973)
abstract: Edgar Melvin Sampson (October 31, 1907 – January 16, 1973), nicknamed "The Lamb", was an American jazz composer, arranger, saxophonist, and violinist. Born in New York City, he began playing violin aged six and picked up the saxophone in high school.

about_quotes.speaker: Benny Waters
about_quotes.evidence: We had Edgar Sampson, the composer of “Stomping at the Savoy,” well he was a friend of mine, see.
about_quotes.doc_title: Benny Waters interviewed by Michael Woods, Los Angeles, California, September 1, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: Bill Coleman
about_quotes.evidence: Later I got to know the other musicians who were Edgar Sampson, alto sax... I had never seen or heard about these musicians but when they started their first number, I knew that it was an orchestra that had something to say... this orchestra was under the leadership of Arthur Gibbs.

about_quotes.speaker: Teddy McRae
about_quotes.evidence: When I joined the band in 1936... Sampson was doing most of the [arranging]... He left the band... in '37 I think he left.

about_quotes.speaker: Charlie Holmes
about_quotes.evidence: Edgar Sampson was in the band. ... Billy was going to take his place. So I reported for rehearsal down there.

about_quotes.speaker: Benny Carter
about_quotes.evidence: I'm trying to remember if Edgar Sampson did any writing for them while I was with him. I'm not sure. Probably later; probably he was with them later on.

about_quotes.speaker: Marian McPartland
about_quotes.evidence: [INTERVIEWER] Fletcher Henderson arrangements, like . . . Edgar Sampson tunes, “Stomping at the Savoy”? [INTERVIEWEE] Yeah, yeah, yeah.
about_quotes.doc_title: Marian McPartland — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
